card.intuition: Each node holds pointers to both its successor and predecessor, enabling O(1) insertion and deletion anywhere — given the node — without traversal.
card.analogy: A conga line where each dancer holds the hands of both the person in front and behind — anyone can step out instantly by having their neighbors grab each other's hands, and a new dancer can step in without reorganizing the whole line.
card.time_complexity: O(1) insert/delete given a node reference; O(n) search
card.space_complexity: O(n) — two pointers per node vs one in singly linked
when_to_use: LRU cache — O(1) node removal from the middle of the list
Deque (double-ended queue) implementation — O(1) push/pop at both ends
Any scenario where you need to delete a node given only its reference, not its predecessor
gotchas: When inserting between nodes A and C, update all four pointers in the right order — setting the new node's prev/next first avoids losing references
When deleting, always check that prev and next are not None (you're not at a sentinel boundary) before updating
Use dummy head and tail sentinels to eliminate edge cases for empty list or end-of-list operations — the LRU cache pattern exploits this

exercise.prompt: Complete the doubly linked list insert_after method. Insert a new node immediately after the given node.
exercise.starter_code:
class DLLNode:
    def __init__(self, val):
        self.val  = val
        self.prev = None
        self.next = None

class DoublyLinkedList:
    def __init__(self):
        self.head = DLLNode(None)  # dummy head
        self.tail = DLLNode(None)  # dummy tail
        self.head.next = self.tail
        self.tail.prev = self.head

    def insert_after(self, node, val):
        """Insert a new node with val immediately after node."""
        new_node      = DLLNode(val)
        new_node.prev = node
        new_node.next = node.next
        node.next.prev = ___________
        node.next      = ___________
        return new_node

    def to_list(self):
        r, c = [], self.head.next
        while c is not self.tail:
            r.append(c.val); c = c.next
        return r
hints: The node after new_node (currently node.next) must point its prev to new_node before you overwrite node.next
Update in this order: new_node.prev, new_node.next, then old_successor.prev, then node.next
exercise.test_code:
dll = DoublyLinkedList()
a = dll.insert_after(dll.head, 1)
b = dll.insert_after(a, 2)
c = dll.insert_after(b, 3)
assert dll.to_list() == [1,2,3], 'Insert three nodes'
mid = dll.insert_after(a, 10)
assert dll.to_list() == [1,10,2,3], 'Insert in middle'
assert mid.prev.val == 1 and mid.next.val == 2, 'Prev/next pointers correct'
print('PASS')
